file_name: Travers et al. - 2019 - Quantifying skin photodamage with spatial frequency domain imaging statistical results.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states in the Abstract and Methods that artificial neural networks (ANNs) were used to characterize statistical data and classify skin photodamage levels (low, medium, high) based on spatial frequency domain imaging (SFDI) parameters. Table 1 provides classification accuracy results for the ANN.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header (e.g., 'Research Article Vol. 10, No. 9 / 1 September 2019'). The content describes an original clinical study involving 55 subjects, detailing specific methods (SFDI imaging, ANN classification), results (classification accuracy of ~70-80%), and discussion of findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Quantifying Skin Photodamage With Spatial Frequency Domain Imaging: Statistical Results
brief_summary: This study investigated the use of multi-wavelength spatial frequency domain imaging (SFDI) to quantify skin photodamage levels in 55 adult subjects with fair skin. Dermatologists rated photodamage on a 0-9 scale, which was then grouped into low, medium, and high grades for comparison with SFDI-derived optical and vascular parameters. Artificial neural networks (ANNs) were employed to classify these photodamage grades based on parameters such as scattering power, total hemoglobin concentration, and deoxy-hemoglobin concentration. The ANN model achieved its best performance using the minimum ensemble approach, yielding validation accuracies of approximately 70%, 76%, and 80% for low, medium, and high-grade lesions, respectively. The results demonstrate that SFDI can effectively discriminate between stages of photodamage, offering a noninvasive tool for risk assessment and monitoring of high-risk populations.

lesion_types_studied.role: Target class / Included in study population
lesion_types_studied.details: The study population included subjects with 'various amounts of photodamage, including clinically-apparent AKs'. The abstract and introduction define the scope as characterizing skin tissue from 'mild photodamage to actinic keratosis (AK)'. The conclusion refers to the study providing maps of 'precancerous lesions such as AK'.

The primary target of the study is quantifying 'skin photodamage' graded by dermatologists into low, medium, and high levels. The study focuses on 'actinic damage' and 'actinic neoplasia' (defined in the text as precancerous actinic keratosis and non-melanoma skin cancer) as the continuum of interest, rather than distinct histological cancer types like BCC or SCC which are mentioned only as potential progressions.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a pathologic effect of UV radiation that can progress from actinic keratosis ('precancerous actinic keratosis (AK), which can progress into basal cell carcinoma (BCC)'). Not a target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a pathologic effect of UV radiation ('progress into ... squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)'). Not a target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: The study uses artificial neural networks (ANNs) to classify skin photodamage into three discrete categories: low (scores ≤3), medium (scores ≥4 and ≤6), and high (scores ≥7). The model predicts these discrete diagnostic categories based on SFDI-derived optical and vascular parameters. Performance is reported as classification accuracy (e.g., 70.3%, 76.3%, 80% for low, medium, and high grades respectively using the minimum ensemble approach).

datasets_used.name: Wright State University Dermatology Clinic Cohort
datasets_used.description: A clinical study cohort of 55 adult subjects (patients in the Wright State University Department of Dermatology clinics) with fair skin (Fitzpatrick scale I or II) and various levels of photodamage, including clinically-apparent actinic keratosis (AK). Bilateral dorsal forearms were imaged, resulting in 110 forearm samples. The dataset includes SFDI-derived optical and vascular parameters and dermatologist ratings of photodamage.

use.training: 60% of the data (66 samples) was used for ANN model generation/training.
use.validation: 20% of the data (22 samples) was sequestered and used to evaluate the final performance of the ANN algorithm.
use.testing: 20% of the data (22 samples) was used for testing during model development.
use.other: The remaining 20% of the initial 110 samples (22 samples) were removed due to inconsistent dermatologist ratings (range >5) before the final split, leaving 88 samples for the ANN split. The dataset was used to characterize skin tissue from mild photodamage to actinic keratosis.

statistical_methods_used.method: Control chart approach with 95% confidence interval
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify and remove inconsistent dermatologist ratings (ranges >5) from the dataset.
To aggregate ratings from 15 dermatologists into a single rating for each arm to reduce grading variance.
To classify skin photodamage levels (low, medium, high) based on SFDI-derived optical and vascular parameters.
To train the ANN model with a goal of 0.001 mean squared error (MSE) over 1000 iterations.
To evaluate the final performance of the ANN algorithm using a sequestered validation set.
To calculate summary statistics for SFDI parameters (absorption, scattering, hemoglobin) and measure tissue heterogeneity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparison of SFDI parameters or ANN classification performance. It reported descriptive statistics (mean, standard deviation) and classification accuracy percentages. A 95% confidence interval was used only for outlier detection (removing inconsistent dermatologist ratings), not for hypothesis testing of the primary research questions.

Wright State University Dermatology Clin.dataset_description: 55 adult subjects (110 dorsal forearms) with fair skin (Fitzpatrick I/II) and varying photodamage levels. Data split: 60% training, 20% testing, 20% validation (sequestered).
Wright State University Dermatology Clin.evaluation_setting: Classification of skin photodamage into three grades: Low (scores ≤3), Medium (scores ≥4 and ≤6), High (scores ≥7) using Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) trained on SFDI-derived optical and vascular parameters.

ANN (Minimum Ensemble Approach).description: Best performing model using the minimum of 15 dermatologist ratings as the ground truth label.
ANN (Minimum Ensemble Approach).notes: Overall best performance. The ANN was always accurate when detecting the high damage group.

ANN (Median Ensemble Approach).description: Using the median of 15 dermatologist ratings as the ground truth label.
ANN (Median Ensemble Approach).notes: Found to be inaccurate overall or to one or more groupings compared to the minimum approach.

ANN (Maximum Ensemble Approach).description: Using the maximum of 15 dermatologist ratings as the ground truth label.
ANN (Maximum Ensemble Approach).notes: Found to be inaccurate overall or to one or more groupings compared to the minimum approach.

ANN (Mode Ensemble Approach).description: Using the mode of 15 dermatologist ratings as the ground truth label.
ANN (Mode Ensemble Approach).notes: Found to be inaccurate overall or to one or more groupings compared to the minimum approach.

Wright State University Dermatology Clin.notes: The study utilized a 3-layer feedforward ANN with 24 log-sigmoidal hidden nodes, trained with scaled conjugate gradient optimization (1000 iterations, goal MSE 0.001). The minimum ensemble approach yielded validation accuracies of approximately 70%, 76%, and 80% for low, medium, and high-grade lesions, respectively.

preprocessing_techniques: Control chart approach with 95% confidence interval to identify and remove inconsistent dermatologist ratings (ranges >5)
Ensemble approaches (minimum, maximum, median, mode) to aggregate ratings from 15 dermatologists into a single rating per arm
Demodulation of three phases to extract spatially modulated component of diffuse reflectance for each frequency
Fitting reflectance data to a 'white Monte Carlo' model to quantify absorption and reduced scattering
Nonlinear algorithm (lsqnonlin, Matlab) to fit scattering at each pixel to a Mie model
Modeling absorption as a linear combination of primary tissue absorbers (HbO2, Hb, melanin, background) to calculate total hemoglobin concentration (THC) and blood oxygenation saturation (StO2)
Random allocation of model development data into training (60%), testing (20%), and validation (20%) sets
conclusions: Optical and vascular parameters derived from multi-wavelength spatial frequency domain imaging (SFDI) can effectively quantify skin photodamage and discriminate between low, medium, and high grades of damage.

The artificial neural network (ANN) classification model achieved its best performance using the minimum ensemble approach, yielding validation accuracies of approximately 70%, 76%, and 80% for low, medium, and high-grade lesions, respectively.
The ANN model was consistently accurate in detecting the high-grade photodamage group.
Ensemble approaches other than the minimum (median, mode, and maximum) were found to be inaccurate for classifying photodamage levels.
SFDI offers a noninvasive approach that clinicians can use for risk assessment and frequent monitoring of high-risk populations for actinic neoplasia.

A limitation of the study was the use of only three wavelengths for multiwavelength fitting, which may lead to less stable fitting and reduced accuracy; future work plans to add more wavelengths to improve quantification, particularly for melanin.
important_remarks: The study population is limited to 55 adult subjects with fair skin (Fitzpatrick scale I or II), restricting generalizability to darker skin types.
The ANN model achieved its best performance using the 'minimum ensemble' approach (validation accuracy ~70-80%); other ensemble methods (median, mode, maximum) were found to be inaccurate.

A key limitation is the use of only three wavelengths (490nm, 590nm, 660nm) for multi-wavelength fitting, which may lead to less stable fitting and reduced accuracy, particularly for quantifying melanin.

Clinical deployment is currently hindered by slow post-processing times (5-10 minutes) performed outside the clinic; faster acquisition and processing techniques (e.g., LUT models) are needed for real-time feedback.
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values) for the primary comparisons, relying instead on descriptive statistics and classification accuracy.
Dermatologist ratings showed significant variance; inconsistent ratings (range >5) were removed using a control chart approach, resulting in the exclusion of 17 observations (255 scores).
The ANN was consistently accurate in detecting the high-grade photodamage group (100% training/testing accuracy), but performance varied for low and medium grades.
No external validation was performed; the dataset was split internally into training (60%), testing (20%), and sequestered validation (20%) sets.
